title: Nicholas Colasanto

infobox.value: Saint Ann Cemetery, [Cranston, Rhode Island](/wiki/Cranston,_Rhode_Island "Cranston, Rhode Island")
[Bryant University](/wiki/Bryant_University "Bryant University") [American Academy of Dramatic Arts](/wiki/American_Academy_of_Dramatic_Arts "American Academy of Dramatic Arts")

(January 19, 1924 – February 12, 1985) was an American actor and television director. He is best known for his role as [Ernie Pantusso](/wiki/Coach_Ernie_Pantusso "Coach Ernie Pantusso") in the American television sitcom *[Cheers](/wiki/Cheers "Cheers")* (1982–1985).

**Nicholas Colasanto** (January 19, 1924 – February 12, 1985) was an American actor and television director. He is best known for his role as [Ernie Pantusso](/wiki/Coach_Ernie_Pantusso "Coach Ernie Pantusso") in the American television sitcom *[Cheers](/wiki/Cheers "Cheers")* (1982–1985).

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nicholas_Colasanto&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Colasanto was born on January 19, 1924, in Providence, Rhode Island,[[1]](#cite_note-1) to Giuseppe "Joseph" Colasanto (1889 – 1944) and Maria "Mary" Colasanto (née Gelfoni; 1889 – 1955), both first-generation [Italian Americans](/wiki/Italian_Americans "Italian Americans").[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) Colasanto attended [Bryant University](/wiki/Bryant_University "Bryant University") (now located in [Smithfield, Rhode Island](/wiki/Smithfield,_Rhode_Island "Smithfield, Rhode Island")[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESnauffer200851-5)) and was a decorated veteran of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), during which he served as a [coxswain](/wiki/Coxswain "Coxswain") in the [United States Navy](/wiki/United_States_Navy "United States Navy").[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Early career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nicholas_Colasanto&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early career")]

By 1951, he was a [bookkeeper](/wiki/Bookkeeper "Bookkeeper").[[7]](#cite_note-EW94-7) Around 1954, he intended to work as an [accountant](/wiki/Accountant "Accountant") for a company in [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia "Saudi Arabia").[[8]](#cite_note-Schenectady-8) Inspired by [Henry Fonda](/wiki/Henry_Fonda "Henry Fonda")'s performance in the Broadway play *[Mister Roberts](/wiki/Mister_Roberts_(play) "Mister Roberts (play)")*, Colasanto applied to the [American Academy of Dramatic Arts](/wiki/American_Academy_of_Dramatic_Arts "American Academy of Dramatic Arts") but was rejected, so he joined a small theater company instead in [Phoenix, Arizona](/wiki/Phoenix,_Arizona "Phoenix, Arizona").[[7]](#cite_note-EW94-7)

## Acting and directing career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nicholas_Colasanto&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Acting and directing career")]

Colasanto is best known for his role as Coach Ernie Pantusso, a character in the television sitcom *Cheers*. His early acting career included a theatrical play *[A Hatful of Rain](/w/index.php?title=A_Hatful_of_Rain_(play)&action=edit&redlink=1 "A Hatful of Rain (play) (page does not exist)")* (1956), starring [Ben Gazzarra](/wiki/Ben_Gazzarra "Ben Gazzarra"), and another role that earned him an [Obie Award](/wiki/Obie_Award "Obie Award") nomination in 1962.[[7]](#cite_note-EW94-7) He also directed episodes of many television series, including *[Hawaii Five-O](/wiki/Hawaii_Five-O_(1968_TV_series) "Hawaii Five-O (1968 TV series)")*,[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERhodes199755–56,_58,_70-9) *[Starsky & Hutch](/wiki/Starsky_%26_Hutch "Starsky & Hutch")*,[[10]](#cite_note-SentinelObituary-10) *[The Streets of San Francisco](/wiki/The_Streets_of_San_Francisco "The Streets of San Francisco")*, *[Bonanza](/wiki/Bonanza "Bonanza")*,[[11]](#cite_note-Leiby-11) *[Columbo](/wiki/Columbo "Columbo")*,[[12]](#cite_note-LAT-12) and *[CHiPs](/wiki/CHiPs "CHiPs")*.[[13]](#cite_note-Mailbag-13) He also appeared in feature films, including *[The Counterfeit Killer](/wiki/The_Counterfeit_Killer "The Counterfeit Killer")* (1968),[[14]](#cite_note-Screen_World-14) *[Fat City](/wiki/Fat_City_(film) "Fat City (film)")* (1972),[[12]](#cite_note-LAT-12) and [Alfred Hitchcock](/wiki/Alfred_Hitchcock "Alfred Hitchcock")'s *[Family Plot](/wiki/Family_Plot "Family Plot")* (1976).[[12]](#cite_note-LAT-12)

Colasanto was in demand as an actor and director, but in the mid-1970s he was diagnosed with [heart disease](/wiki/Heart_disease "Heart disease"), which was exacerbated by his [alcoholism](/wiki/Alcoholism "Alcoholism").[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESnauffer200852-15) After twenty years of alcoholism, he became an active member of [Alcoholics Anonymous](/wiki/Alcoholics_Anonymous "Alcoholics Anonymous") from March 31, 1976,[[8]](#cite_note-Schenectady-8) and became sober in the same year.[[16]](#cite_note-alcoholism-16) In the late 1970s, he began having difficulty securing directing jobs as his health was declining. His last major film role was as mob boss Tommy Como in *[Raging Bull](/wiki/Raging_Bull "Raging Bull")* (1980).[[17]](#cite_note-17)

Colasanto was preparing to retire when the role of [Coach Ernie Pantusso](/wiki/Coach_Ernie_Pantusso "Coach Ernie Pantusso") was offered to him on *Cheers*. Coach would become his best known role.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESnauffer200852-15) By the [third season](/wiki/Cheers_(season_3) "Cheers (season 3)") of *Cheers*, Colasanto's health had seriously deteriorated. His fellow cast members noticed his weight loss, but Colasanto kept the severity of his illness secret.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESnauffer200855-18) Shortly after the Christmas holiday in 1984, he was admitted to a local hospital for water in his lungs.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESnauffer200855-18) Co-star [Ted Danson](/wiki/Ted_Danson "Ted Danson") later said Colasanto had difficulty remembering his lines during production of the season.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESnauffer200855-18)

When Colasanto was released from the hospital in the week of January 28 – February 3, 1985, after a two-week stay,[[19]](#cite_note-Feb85Montreal-19) his doctor recommended he should not return to work.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESnauffer200856-20) Although he appeared in the [cold opening](/wiki/Cold_open "Cold open") of the third-season finale episode "Rescue Me" (1985),[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESnauffer200856-20) Colasanto's last full episode was "Cheerio Cheers" (1985), which was filmed in late November 1984.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

## Death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nicholas_Colasanto&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Death")]

Colasanto died of a [heart attack](/wiki/Heart_attack "Heart attack") at his home on February 12, 1985, at the age of 61.[[12]](#cite_note-LAT-12)[[22]](#cite_note-22) A memorial service was held in [North Hollywood, Los Angeles](/wiki/North_Hollywood,_Los_Angeles "North Hollywood, Los Angeles"), and attended by the full staff and cast of *Cheers*.[[23]](#cite_note-23) In addition, over 300 mourners, including castmate [John Ratzenberger](/wiki/John_Ratzenberger "John Ratzenberger"), attended the February 16 funeral Mass at Holy Cross Church in Providence.[[24]](#cite_note-funeral-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25) Colasanto is buried in Saint Ann Cemetery in [Cranston, Rhode Island](/wiki/Cranston,_Rhode_Island "Cranston, Rhode Island").[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESnauffer200857-26)

On April 19, 1985, Colasanto was posthumously awarded the Best Supporting Actor by [Viewers for Quality Television](/wiki/Viewers_for_Quality_Television "Viewers for Quality Television"), a non-profit organization that determined what was considered high-quality on television.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

Colasanto's character was written out of the show as also having died. The fourth-season premiere episode, "Birth, Death, Love and Rice" (1985), deals with Coach's death and introduces Colasanto's successor [Woody Harrelson](/wiki/Woody_Harrelson "Woody Harrelson"), who played [Woody Boyd](/wiki/Woody_Boyd "Woody Boyd").[[28]](#cite_note-DVD_cheers_birth_death-28) Colasanto had hung a picture of [Geronimo](/wiki/Geronimo "Geronimo") in his dressing room. After his death, the picture was placed on the wall in the bar of the *Cheers* production set in his memory. Near the end of the [final episode](/wiki/One_for_the_Road_(Cheers) "One for the Road (Cheers)") of *Cheers* in 1993, eight years after Colasanto's death, bar owner [Sam Malone](/wiki/Sam_Malone "Sam Malone") ([Ted Danson](/wiki/Ted_Danson "Ted Danson")) walks over to the picture and straightens it.[[29]](#cite_note-geronimo-29)

## Selected filmography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nicholas_Colasanto&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Selected filmography")]

### Film

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nicholas_Colasanto&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Film")]

* *[The Counterfeit Killer](/wiki/The_Counterfeit_Killer "The Counterfeit Killer")* (1968)[[14]](#cite_note-Screen_World-14) – Plainclothesman
* *[Fat City](/wiki/Fat_City_(film) "Fat City (film)")* (1972)[[12]](#cite_note-LAT-12) – Ruben
* *[The Manchu Eagle Murder Caper Mystery](/wiki/The_Manchu_Eagle_Murder_Caper_Mystery "The Manchu Eagle Murder Caper Mystery")* (1975)[[30]](#cite_note-30) – Bert
* *[Family Plot](/wiki/Family_Plot "Family Plot")* (1976)[[12]](#cite_note-LAT-12) – Constantine
* *[Raging Bull](/wiki/Raging_Bull "Raging Bull")* (1980) – Tommy Como, mob boss

### Television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nicholas_Colasanto&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Television")]

#### Actor

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nicholas_Colasanto&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Actor")]

* *[Cheers](/wiki/Cheers "Cheers")* (1982–1985) – [Coach Ernie Pantusso](/wiki/Coach_Ernie_Pantusso "Coach Ernie Pantusso"), co-bartender (final appearance)
* *[The Streets of San Francisco](/wiki/The_Streets_of_San_Francisco "The Streets of San Francisco")*[[7]](#cite_note-EW94-7) – "Deathwatch" (1973)
* *[Baretta](/wiki/Baretta "Baretta")* – "The Secret Of Terry Lake" (1975)[[31]](#cite_note-31)

#### Director

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nicholas_Colasanto&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Director")]

* *[Run for Your Life](/wiki/Run_for_Your_Life_(TV_series) "Run for Your Life (TV series)")* (1965–1968)[[8]](#cite_note-Schenectady-8)
* *[Ironside](/wiki/Ironside_(1967_TV_series) "Ironside (1967 TV series)")* – "The Challenge" (1968)[[32]](#cite_note-32)
* *[Hawaii Five-O](/wiki/Hawaii_Five-O_(1968_TV_series) "Hawaii Five-O (1968 TV series)")* – "A Thousand Pardons, You're Dead" (1969),[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERhodes199755-33) "To Hell with Babe Ruth" (1969),[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERhodes199756-34) "Just Lucky, I Guess" (1969),[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERhodes199758-35) and "Most Likely to Murder" (1970)[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERhodes199770-36)
* *[Bonanza](/wiki/Bonanza "Bonanza")* – "Ambush at Rio Lobo" (1972)[[11]](#cite_note-Leiby-11)
* *[Hec Ramsey](/wiki/Hec_Ramsey "Hec Ramsey")*[[37]](#cite_note-37) – "The Detroit Connection" (1973)
* *[Columbo](/wiki/Columbo "Columbo")* – "Étude in Black" (1972)[[12]](#cite_note-LAT-12) and "Swan Song" (1974)[[12]](#cite_note-LAT-12)
* *[Nakia](/wiki/Nakia_(TV_series) "Nakia (TV series)")* (1974) – "No Place to Hide"[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBjorklund39-38) and "A Matter of Choice"[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBjorklund39-38)
* *[Logan's Run](/wiki/Logan%27s_Run_(TV_series) "Logan's Run (TV series)")* – "Man Out of Time" (1977)[[39]](#cite_note-39)
* *[The Name of the Game](/wiki/The_Name_of_the_Game_(TV_series) "The Name of the Game (TV series)")*[[19]](#cite_note-Feb85Montreal-19)
* *[Starsky & Hutch](/wiki/Starsky_%26_Hutch "Starsky & Hutch")*[[10]](#cite_note-SentinelObituary-10)
* *[The Streets of San Francisco](/wiki/The_Streets_of_San_Francisco "The Streets of San Francisco")*[[13]](#cite_note-Mailbag-13)
* *[CHiPs](/wiki/CHiPs "CHiPs")*[[13]](#cite_note-Mailbag-13)
